GPU version of `scipy.*` functions in `cupyx.scipy.*` namespace.

This is a tracker issue that summarizes the implementation status of each SciPy public module in CuPy. See the [comparison table](https://docs.cupy.dev/en/latest/reference/comparison.html#scipy-cupy-apis) for details.

## Legends

* 🥚 Module not yet available in CuPy.
* 🐥 Module available in CuPy but coverage needs improvement.
* :shipit: Module available in CuPy and major features are implemented (may still have a few unimplemented APIs).
* ❌ Out of scope.

## List of Modules

- 🥚 Clustering package (`scipy.cluster`)
    - 🥚 K-means clustering and vector quantization (`scipy.cluster.vq`) (#5947)
    - 🥚 Hierarchical clustering (`scipy.cluster.hierarchy`) (#3434)
- 🥚 Constants (`scipy.constants`)
- :shipit: Discrete Fourier transforms (`scipy.fft`)
- :shipit: Legacy discrete Fourier transforms (`scipy.fftpack`) (note: deprecated, no further development planned)
- 🥚 Integration and ODEs (`scipy.integrate`) (#7019)
- :shipit: Interpolation (`scipy.interpolate`) (#7186)
- 🥚 Input and output (`scipy.io`)
    - 🥚 `scipy.io.arff`
    - 🥚 `scipy.io.matlab`
    - 🥚 `scipy.io.wavfile`
- 🐥 Linear algebra (`scipy.linalg`)
    - 🥚 Low-level BLAS functions (`scipy.linalg.blas`)
    - ❌ `scipy.linalg.cython_blas`
    - 🥚 Low-level LAPACK functions (`scipy.linalg.lapack`) c.f. [`cupyx.lapack`](https://github.com/cupy/cupy/blob/main/cupyx/lapack.py)
    - ❌ `scipy.linalg.cython_lapack`
    - 🥚 Interpolative matrix decomposition (`scipy.linalg.interpolative`)
- :shipit: Multidimensional image processing (`scipy.ndimage`)
- 🥚 Orthogonal distance regression (`scipy.odr`)
- 🥚 Optimization and root finding (`scipy.optimize`) (#6112)
- :shipit: Signal processing (`scipy.signal`) (#7403)
    - :shipit: `scipy.signal.windows` (#7404)
- :shipit: Sparse matrices (`scipy.sparse`)
    - :shipit: Sparse linear algebra (`scipy.sparse.linalg`)
    - 🐥 Compressed sparse graph routines (`scipy.sparse.csgraph`) (#2431)
- :shipit: Spatial algorithms and data structures (`scipy.spatial`) (#5946)
    - 🐥 Distance computations (`scipy.spatial.distance`) (#5946)
    - 🥚 Spatial transformations (`scipy.spatial.transform`)
- 🐥 Special functions (`scipy.special`) (note: planned to be backed by SciPy in future #8163)
- 🐥 Statistical functions (`scipy.stats`)
    - 🥚 Contingency table functions (`scipy.stats.contingency`) (note: RAFT could be used)
    - 🥚 `scipy.stats.distributions`
    - 🥚 Statistical functions for masked arrays (`scipy.stats.mstats`) (note: masked arrays unsupported in CuPy)
    - 🥚 Quasi-Monte Carlo submodule (`scipy.stats.qmc`)
    - 🥚 Random Number Generators (`scipy.stats.sampling`) (note: RAFT could be used)

**Note:** [Several modules in SciPy](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/dev/api-dev/array_api.html#currently-supported-functionality) such as `scipy.cluster.hierarchy` accepts CuPy ndarrays as inputs through the Array API standard.

### Starter Task

If you are new to CuPy and wondering where to start, here are some good first things to try. They are relatively simple and independent.

* APIs under [Statistical Functions](https://docs.cupy.dev/en/latest/reference/comparison.html#statistical-functions) (`scipy.stats`)
* `scipy.sparse.block_diag` (#7058)
* `scipy.sparse.load_npz`
* `scipy.sparse.save_npz`
* `scipy.constants.*` (hint: most things except for methods can just be borrowed from SciPy)

## Steps to Contribute

1. Fork and star :star: the CuPy repository :wink:

1. Pick a function you want to work on from any of the modules listed above. You can find the function in the [SciPy API Reference](https://scipy.github.io/devdocs/reference/index.html) to understand what should be implemented. If you want to work on new modules (🥚), first discuss with us on this issue.

1. Implement a function in your branch. If you need help, join [Gitter](https://gitter.im/cupy/community) or just ask for help in this issue.

1. Don't forget to write a test code!

1. Build CuPy and run tests to confirm that the function runs fine:
  `pip install -e . && pytest tests/cupyx_tests/scipy_tests/PATH_TO_YOUR_TEST`
   See the [Contribution Guide](https://docs.cupy.dev/en/latest/contribution.html#unit-testing) for details.

1. Submit a pull-request to the `main` branch.

Note that you will need a GPU environment to develop CuPy.

Maybe I misunderstood what you mean't by 'wrap'... If I try to pass cupy arrays into permutation test, it immediately complains:

    TypeError: Implicit conversion to a NumPy array is not allowed. Please use `.get()` to construct a NumPy array explicitly.

Seems there is no speed improvement over numpy on simple 'wrapped' statistics:

```python
def mean_ind_cp(x: cp.ndarray, y: cp.ndarray, axis=0):
    cp_x = cp.array(x)
    cp_y = cp.array(y)

    return cp.mean(cp_x, axis=axis) - cp.mean(cp_y, axis=axis)

between the sum and the mean permutation statistic when the cases and

On Wed, 2 Oct 2024, 3:53 pm Matt Haberland, ***@***.***>
wrote:

> It's just history - the permutation t-test was requested first, so it was
> added, then it was obvious that a general function would be useful.
> I think the exact test (with small enough samples) will give the same
> results. Randomized tests might give sightly different results even with
> the same randomized state. The main conceptual difference is the two-sided
> p-value. The permutation t-test, IIRC, counts the elements of the
> permutation distribution with absolute value greater than the observed
> statistic. permutation_test doubles the minimum of the two one-sided
> p-values. They are both common conventions for two-sided p-values. The
> absolute value only works when the null distribution is expected to be
> symmetric, so it couldn't be used for the more general permutation_test.
> @steppi <https://github.com/steppi> pointed out that the doubling of the
> smaller p-value can be interpreted as a Bonferroni correction when testing
> multiple hypotheses testing (it's equivalent to performing both one-sided
> tests and halving your threshold for significance).

When running `mypy` on the scipy directory `mypy scipy/`, I get the following errors:

```none
scipy/stats/setup.py:48: error: Cannot find implementation or library stub for module named '_generate_pyx'  [import]
scipy/sparse/tests/test_sparsetools.py:164: error: List item 0 has incompatible type "ParameterSet"; expected "Sequence[Collection[object]]"  [list-item]

scipy/stats/_discrete_distns.py:1691: error: Incompatible types in assignment (expression has type "str", base class "_nchypergeom_gen" defined the type as "None")  [assignment]
scipy/stats/tests/test_qmc.py:10: error: Skipping analyzing 'scipy.stats._sobol': found module but no type hints or library stubs  [import]
scipy/stats/tests/test_stats.py:3848: error: Module has no attribute "norm"  [attr-defined]
scipy/stats/tests/test_stats.py:3849: error: Module has no attribute "norm"  [attr-defined]
Found 13 errors in 7 files (checked 664 source files)
```

### Proposed Solution

I have tried to discern some of these errors but would love input from someone more experienced with `mypy` and typing!

For the first error:

```none
scipy/stats/setup.py:48: error: Cannot find implementation or library stub for module named '_generate_pyx'  [import]
```

stubs are yet to be added and so we can just `# type: ignore[import]` at that line in `scipy/stats/setup.py`.

Also, the three errors in `scipy/sparse/tests/test_sparsetools.py` are false positives:

```none
scipy/sparse/tests/test_sparsetools.py:164: error: List item 0 has incompatible type "ParameterSet"; expected "Sequence[Collection[object]]"  [list-item]
scipy/sparse/tests/test_sparsetools.py:165: error: List item 1 has incompatible type "ParameterSet"; expected "Sequence[Collection[object]]"  [list-item]
scipy/sparse/tests/test_sparsetools.py:169: error: List item 5 has incompatible type "ParameterSet"; expected "Sequence[Collection[object]]"  [list-item]
```

One possible fix for this is to just ignore them as no stubs have been added yet for the sparse submodule:

```none
[mypy-scipy.sparse.tests.test_sparsetools]
ignore_errors = True
```

Errors occurring in `scipy/special/_precompute` are due to missing stubs of `sympy` and `mpmath`.

```none
scipy/special/_precompute/wright_bessel_data.py:15: error: Skipping analyzing 'mpmath': found module but no type hints or library stubs  [import]
scipy/special/_precompute/wright_bessel.py:13: error: Skipping analyzing 'sympy': found module but no type hints or library stubs  [import]
scipy/special/_precompute/wright_bessel.py:13: note: See https://mypy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/running_mypy.html#missing-imports
scipy/special/_precompute/wright_bessel.py:16: error: Skipping analyzing 'sympy.polys.polyfuncs': found module but no type hints or library stubs  [import]
```

Many places ignore them (as they don't have stubs yet). The same can be done here by adding a `# type: ignore[import]`.

Stubs for `biasedurn` don't yet exist and hence the error:

```none
scipy/stats/_discrete_distns.py:17: error: Skipping analyzing 'scipy.stats.biasedurn': found module but no type hints or library stubs  [import]
```

The extension module `biasedurn` was added in #13330. Probably, authors forgot to add the extension to the ignore list in `mypy.ini`.

```none
[mypy-scipy.stats.biasedurn]
ignore_missing_imports = True
```

The other errors in `scipy/stats/_discrete_distns.py` also seem related to #13330: 

```none
scipy/stats/_discrete_distns.py:1608: error: Incompatible types in assignment (expression has type "str", base class "_nchypergeom_gen" defined the type as "None")  [assignment]
scipy/stats/_discrete_distns.py:1691: error: Incompatible types in assignment (expression has type "str", base class "_nchypergeom_gen" defined the type as "None")  [assignment]
```

These are only re-assignment errors (common is python) and hence can be ignored:

```none
[mypy-scipy.stats._discrete_distns]
ignore_errors = True
```

The one error in `scipy/stats/tests/test_qmc.py` is because mypy cannot find stubs for `scipy.stats._sobol`:

```none
scipy/stats/tests/test_qmc.py:10: error: Skipping analyzing 'scipy.stats._sobol': found module but no type hints or library stubs  [import]
```

I don't think stubs exist for `_sobol` currently. Maybe, ignore them using `# type: ignore[import]` or add `scipy.stats._sobol` to ignore list in `mypy.ini`:

```none
[mypy-scipy.stats._sobol]
ignore_missing_imports = True
```

I don't understand the last two errors:

```none
scipy/stats/tests/test_stats.py:3848: error: Module has no attribute "norm"  [attr-defined]
scipy/stats/tests/test_stats.py:3849: error: Module has no attribute "norm"  [attr-defined]
```

Somehow, `mypy` can't find `norm` in `scipy.stats`! Many other places use `stats.norm` but this error doesn't occur at those places... I don't know why this happens here only, but adding `# type: ignore` there should fix it (not sure if we should do that though...).

### Other Thoughts

I don't think there is a CI run for `mypy` currently (sorry if there is!). Should `mypy` be added to CI pipelines or wait for more stubs to be added? Adding a basic CI run to ensure that new features are tracked in the `mypy.ini` ignore lists would be helpful when adding stubs for it later. ## Legends

* 🥚 Module not yet available in CuPy.
* 🐥 Module available in CuPy but coverage needs improvement.
* :shipit: Module available in CuPy and major features are implemented (may still have a few unimplemented APIs).
* ❌ Out of scope.

## List of Modules

- 🥚 Clustering package (`scipy.cluster`)
    - 🥚 K-means clustering and vector quantization (`scipy.cluster.vq`) (#5947)
    - 🥚 Hierarchical clustering (`scipy.cluster.hierarchy`) (#3434)
- 🥚 Constants (`scipy.constants`)
- :shipit: Discrete Fourier transforms (`scipy.fft`)
- :shipit: Legacy discrete Fourier transforms (`scipy.fftpack`) (note: deprecated, no further development planned)
- 🥚 Integration and ODEs (`scipy.integrate`) (#7019)
- :shipit: Interpolation (`scipy.interpolate`) (#7186)
- 🥚 Input and output (`scipy.io`)
    - 🥚 `scipy.io.arff`
    - 🥚 `scipy.io.matlab`
    - 🥚 `scipy.io.wavfile`
- 🐥 Linear algebra (`scipy.linalg`)
    - 🥚 Low-level BLAS functions (`scipy.linalg.blas`)
    - ❌ `scipy.linalg.cython_blas`
    - 🥚 Low-level LAPACK functions (`scipy.linalg.lapack`) c.f. [`cupyx.lapack`](https://github.com/cupy/cupy/blob/main/cupyx/lapack.py)
    - ❌ `scipy.linalg.cython_lapack`
    - 🥚 Interpolative matrix decomposition (`scipy.linalg.interpolative`)
- :shipit: Multidimensional image processing (`scipy.ndimage`)
- 🥚 Orthogonal distance regression (`scipy.odr`)
- 🥚 Optimization and root finding (`scipy.optimize`) (#6112)
- :shipit: Signal processing (`scipy.signal`) (#7403)
    - :shipit: `scipy.signal.windows` (#7404)
- :shipit: Sparse matrices (`scipy.sparse`)
    - :shipit: Sparse linear algebra (`scipy.sparse.linalg`)
    - 🐥 Compressed sparse graph routines (`scipy.sparse.csgraph`) (#2431)
- :shipit: Spatial algorithms and data structures (`scipy.spatial`) (#5946)
    - 🐥 Distance computations (`scipy.spatial.distance`) (#5946)
    - 🥚 Spatial transformations (`scipy.spatial.transform`)
- 🐥 Special functions (`scipy.special`) (note: planned to be backed by SciPy in future #8163)
- 🐥 Statistical functions (`scipy.stats`)
    - 🥚 Contingency table functions (`scipy.stats.contingency`) (note: RAFT could be used)
    - 🥚 `scipy.stats.distributions`
    - 🥚 Statistical functions for masked arrays (`scipy.stats.mstats`) (note: masked arrays unsupported in CuPy)
    - 🥚 Quasi-Monte Carlo submodule (`scipy.stats.qmc`)
    - 🥚 Random Number Generators (`scipy.stats.sampling`) (note: RAFT could be used)

**Note:** [Several modules in SciPy](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/dev/api-dev/array_api.html#currently-supported-functionality) such as `scipy.cluster.hierarchy` accepts CuPy ndarrays as inputs through the Array API standard.

### Starter Task

If you are new to CuPy and wondering where to start, here are some good first things to try. They are relatively simple and independent.

* APIs under [Statistical Functions](https://docs.cupy.dev/en/latest/reference/comparison.html#statistical-functions) (`scipy.stats`)
* `scipy.sparse.block_diag` (#7058)
* `scipy.sparse.load_npz`
* `scipy.sparse.save_npz`
* `scipy.constants.*` (hint: most things except for methods can just be borrowed from SciPy)

## Steps to Contribute

1. Fork and star :star: the CuPy repository :wink:

1. Pick a function you want to work on from any of the modules listed above. You can find the function in the [SciPy API Reference](https://scipy.github.io/devdocs/reference/index.html) to understand what should be implemented. If you want to work on new modules (🥚), first discuss with us on this issue.

1. Implement a function in your branch. If you need help, join [Gitter](https://gitter.im/cupy/community) or just ask for help in this issue.

1. Don't forget to write a test code!

1. Build CuPy and run tests to confirm that the function runs fine:
  `pip install -e . && pytest tests/cupyx_tests/scipy_tests/PATH_TO_YOUR_TEST`
   See the [Contribution Guide](https://docs.cupy.dev/en/latest/contribution.html#unit-testing) for details.

1. Submit a pull-request to the `main` branch.

Note that you will need a GPU environment to develop CuPy.
